text: The video shows a sixteen-centimeter long uterus with two visible fibroids, highlighting the size and presence of these growths within the uterine tissue.
The uterus is shown with two fibroids, one located on the anterior wall and the other on the posterior wall.
The surgeon uses the Enseal device to perform tissue sealing during the total laparoscopic hysterectomy procedure.
The surgeon coagulates the round ligament approximately three centimeters lateral to the uterus using a surgical instrument.

The surgeon utilizes two ipsilateral ports and one contralateral port for the laparoscopic hysterectomy. In the left hand, a bipolar Maryland forceps is visible, while the right hand holds an Enseal device.
The surgeon uses the Enseal device to coagulate the round ligament, then cuts the remaining portion of the ligament near the uterus.
The surgeon carefully opens the anterior leaf of the broad ligament, revealing the fallopian tube.
The surgeon uses the Enseal device to dissect and cut the remaining portion of the major salping. The ovarian ligament is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon separates the ovarian ligament while preserving the ovary, using surgical instruments visible in the ipsilateral and contralateral ports.
The surgeon opens the posterior leaf of the broad ligament, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.

The surgeon moves to the left side of the surgical field, where the sigmoid colon is visible on the extreme left. Approximately four centimeters from the uterus, the round ligament on the left side is dissected.
The surgeon opens the NCJ leaf of the broad ligament, continuing the dissection process on the left side of the surgical field.
The surgeon directs dissection towards the cervix and uterovesical fold, carefully separating the anterior peritoneum. The fallopian tube is visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon uses a bipolar device to coagulate tissue at two locations, followed by cutting between these sites with scissors. The bipolar device may cause some collateral damage to surrounding tissues.
The surgeon identifies and isolates the ovarian ligament, visible adjacent to the dissected round ligament on the left side.
The surgeon carefully opens the anterior peritoneum at the uterovesical (UV) fold, gradually separating the tissue layers.

The surgeon separates the bladder from the uterus, which is retroverted, using a uterine manipulator to push the colpotomizer for easier separation. The posterior peritoneum is opened just above the utero sacral ligament, with the uterus now introverted.
The surgeon opens both leaves of the peritoneum to better expose the artery, facilitating easier cooking with the bipolar device.
The uterine artery is visible, showing the knuckle of the artery. The peritoneum is being dissected below the artery.
The surgeon demonstrates the use of an Ensealer device to dissect tissue, with an option for suturing if desired.
The surgeon has separated both the posterior and anterior peritoneum at the level of the uterus. The Enseal device is used to seal the entire vascular bundle visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon applies the Enseal or Ligasure device twice to securely seal the uterine artery, ensuring effective hemostasis.
The uterine artery is sealed and cut using a surgical device. A clamp is applied near the cervix, and the device is used twice for secure sealing.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the vascular pedicle on the left side using an articulating Enseal device, which allows precise manipulation to bring the instrument closer to the cervix.
The surgeon performs a second attempt at coagulating the uterine pedicle, applying coagulation two or three times before cutting the tissue.
The surgeon applies an additional clamp to the veins and proceeds to dissect the remaining parametrium tissue, ensuring complete separation from surrounding structures.

The surgeon secures the vessels on either side and inserts the uterine manipulator colpotomizer deeper. Using a harmonic scalpel, the surgeon begins the colpotomy by cutting around the colpotomizer cup. The lateral fornix is opened, revealing the plastic tip of the colpotomizer.

The surgeon completes the anterior colpotomy and proceeds to the posterior colpotomy using a harmonic device. The colpotomy ring is maintained to ensure precision and prevent lateral deviation. The opened vagina is visible laterally as the surgeon moves posteriorly and towards the right side.
The surgeon uses the harmonic scalpel to cut the right lateral attachments of the uterus, completing the dissection on the right side.
The vaginal vault is visible with the cervix held by a tenaculum. The uterus is being pulled through the vagina, with potential bisection or morcellation using two vaginal speculums.
The surgeon removes the bisected uterus vaginally, using a speculum to protect the bladder and another speculum positioned below to protect the rectum during the extraction process.

The surgeon takes a bite through the anterior and posterior layers of the vagina, including the vaginal fascia and epithelium, using an extracorporeal knot technique. The knot is tied and then cut above the knot using scissors.
The surgeon sutures the angles of the vagina using a vicryloid PDS suture, approximating the uterosacral ligament with the vaginal angles.
The surgeon approximates both angles of the vagina with the uterosacral ligaments, using sutures to secure these structures together.

The surgeon uses an extracorporeal knot to approximate the vaginal vault, feeding the suture to prevent shearing. An extracorporeal square knot is applied, followed by past pointing to lock the knot multiple times. The previous and current sutures are tied together using intracorporeal knots, effectively closing the vaginal vault.
